# A Holistic Approach to Predicting the FIFA World Cup 2022 

By Maria Eduarda Santana

*This paper proposes a framework to predict the 2022 FIFA World Cup using Poisson Regressions and Monte Carlo simulations. The framework simulates the World Cup from the qualifying stages to the tournament itself, and adds to the soccer literature by introducing Monte Carlo simulations to the group draw, which allows us to make earlier predictions before having the group allocation. Results indicate Brazil, Belgium, and France to be the favorite contestants for the upcoming championship.*

1. # Introduction

   

The FIFA World Cup is one of the most televised events around the world. In 2018, over three billion people watched the tournament[^1], which is an audience thirty times larger than the Super Bowl’s. Driven by the tournament media attention and its economic impact, extensive research has been conducted on predictions for the World Cup rounds (Goldman 2014; Groll 2015). Regarding the tournament economic impact, Goldman Sachs’ research (2014) indicates that World Cup winners usually outperform the global market by 3.5% in the first month after the cup, which can be valuable information for portfolio management.  
The soccer literature traditionally uses Poisson Regressions and Monte Carlo simulations to predict the tournament, and the simulations are done after the FIFA group draws which, for the 2022 World Cup, will happen in April 2022\. This paper, in addition to using Poisson Regressions and Monte Carlo simulations, aims to add the qualifiers and the group draw phase into the simulation framework. This allows for earlier predictions \- about 6 months earlier than what the literature provides with the current framework \- of the likelihood of each team moving to the following tournament round.  
The first part of this paper will go through FIFA World Cup process, the second part will go through a data overview, and the third part will go through the proposed framework. The proposed framework will be broken down into the simulation blocks: the qualifiers, the group draw, and the tournament itself. Finally, this framework will be validated using 2018’s World Cup and will be used for 2022 World Cup predictions. 

2. # FIFA World Cup Process 

This regulation section is analogous to the previous research done in World Cup 2014 Predictions using Poisson Regressions and Monte Carlo Simulations. There are 210 FIFA members, but the qualifying process selects the 32 teams that will play in the tournament. The qualifying process is separately organized by the regional federations of each continent, and each regional federation has a pre-defined number of spots available (with the option of qualifying more teams depending on the intercontinental playoffs). After the qualifying process, the 32 teams are divided into eight groups (A \- H) and the tournament follows a sequence of group stage and the knockout stages. In the group stage, ties are allowed and every team of the group plays each other; points are then calculated to define the two teams that will move to the knockout stages. Afterward, in the knockout stages, the winners of the groups play the runner-ups of other groups and the matches are eliminatory (no ties allowed). These knockout stages comprise the round 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals, the third-place match, and the final.  
Figure I illustrates the process from the qualifiers to the final: the blue section indicates the qualifying stage with the five major confederations, the red section indicates the group draw that splits the 32 teams into the eight groups from A \- H, and the green section illustrates the tournament with its stages. The figure also illustrates how this overall structure was organized into an algorithm: the matches results from each round are predicted using Poisson Regressions (dotted green lines), and the overall loop represents the Monte Carlo simulation. The loop starts with the estimation of the qualifiers, creates a pseudo group draw, and based on this pseudo group draw predicts all the tournament matches until the final. After reaching a final result, the simulation goes back to the qualifiers and re-starts the loop to simulate a new tournament. This  framework will be further detailed in the Analysis Framework section.  
![][image1]  
**Figure I.** World Cup overview from qualifiers to the tournament[^2]

3. # Data Overview 

   

Three major data sources were used to design the simulation framework: FIFA rankings, Elo rankings, and World Cup match data from 1960 to 2018\. Each data source will be detailed in the following paragraphs.  
The FIFA rankings, which can be retrieved from FIFA’s website, are a proxy for the quality of the teams. Until 2018, they were calculated as an average of game points for each team over a time period. However, in 2018, FIFA changed its ranking calculation to make it analogous to Elo rankings. Now, the ranking is calculated in a window format: adding and subtracting points from a previous total, rather than calculating a total average. These raking points were used in two parts of the simulation: first, to estimate the 32 teams that would qualify for the World Cup, and second, to split the pots of the group draw (following the exact same procedure FIFA uses to divide the pots based on the FIFA ranking).  
The Elo rankings, which can be retrieved from Elo’s website, are analogously a proxy for the quality of the teams. However, they are significantly older. FIFA’s rankings started being calculated in 1992, while Elo rankings can be retrieved for soccer since the early 30s, making it the optimal ranking method to be used for earlier World Cups. The Elo rankings were used as explanatory variables for the Poisson Regression, and were a significant predictor of match winner, indicating how the overall quality of the team is, expectedly, a large indicator of its winning probability.   
Finally, World Cup match data from 1960 \- 2018, which can also be retrieved from FIFA’s website, was used to calculate explanatory variables to train the Poisson Model. Once the outcome of the Poisson model is the number of goals each team scored in a match, relevant explanatory variables can be retrieved from historical data, such as the average number of goals the team has scored in the previous year, the number of goals the opponent has taken in the previous year, and whether the team had a home continent advantage for that match. The explanatory variables of the model will be further detailed in the Poisson Model section. 

4. # Analysis Framework 

As originally described in Figure I, the holistic approach to predicting the 2022 World Cup is based on breaking down the simulation into three main blocks: the qualifying stage, the group draws, and the tournament. 

1. ## Qualifiers 

   

	Each confederation has a specific process for qualifying the teams, for instance, UEFA follows a process where it splits the 55 European nations into 10 groups and the winners of the groups automatically qualify for the World Cup (with the other 3 spots defined through playoffs)[^3]. Meanwhile, for CONMEBOL qualifiers, the 10 South American countries play each other and the top four are automatically qualified.  
The intricacies of each confederation qualifying process and the fact that this process frequently changes pose a challenge to simulating this stage. However, although each confederation follows a different procedure, we have found that a good proxy for the 32 countries that will ultimately qualify for the World Cup is the FIFA ranking of the previous year.   
Table I shows the top FIFA-ranked countries for each confederation in 2017, and the countries that qualified for the 2018 World Cup. The table shows how 26 out of the 32 qualified teams could’ve been predicted by the FIFA ranking of the previous year[^4]. As a result, the FIFA ranking will be used as a proxy to estimate the final list of teams qualified for the 2022 World Cup in Qatar.

**Table I.** Top 2017 FIFA ranked countries[^5] and qualified teams for 2018 World Cup

| UEFA (14 teams) |  |  | CAF (5 teams) |  |  | CONMEBOL (5 teams) |  |
| ----- | :---- | ----- | ----- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| Top FIFA 2017 | Qualified 2018 |  | Top FIFA 2017 | Qualified 2018 |  | Top FIFA 2017 | Qualified 2018 |
| Germany | Russia |  | Tunisia | Tunisia |  | Brazil | Brazil |
| Portugal | France |  | Egypt | Nigeria |  | Argentina | Uruguay |
| Belgium | Portugal |  | Senegal | Morocco |  | Chile | Argentina |
| Poland | Germany |  | Congo | Senegal |  | Peru | Colombia |
| France | Serbia |  | Nigeria | Egypt |  | Colombia | Peru |
| Spain | Poland |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Switzerland | England |  | **AFC (5 teams)** |  |  | **CONCACAF (3 teams)** |  |
| England | Spain |  | Top FIFA 2017 | Qualified 2018 |  | Top FIFA 2017 | Qualified 2018 |
| Wales | Belgium |  | Iran | Iran |  | Mexico | Mexico |
| Italy | Iceland |  | Australia | Japan |  | Costa Rica | Costa Rica |
| Croatia | Switzerland |  | Japan | South Korea |  | United States | Panama |
| Denmark | Croatia |  | China | South Arabia |  |  |  |
| Netherlands | Denmark |  | South Korea | Australia |  |  |  |
| Iceland | Sweden |  |  |  |  |  |  |

2. ## Group Draw 

	The group draw stage is described in Figure II, with the number of teams in each section indicated in bold. After estimating the final list of the 32 teams that will qualify for the 2022 World Cup, this list has to be divided into four pots based on the FIFA rankings (the strongest teams and the host in Pot 1 and the weakest teams in Pot 4). This follows the FIFA group draw event that will be televised in April 2022\.   
After the pots are divided, a randomized group draw can be created. The process works as follows: from Pot 1, teams for groups A \- H are drawn, and then, from Pot 2, teams for groups A \- H are drawn, until we reach Pot 4\. This is a way to balance the groups such that all groups have a balance of stronger and weaker teams. However, this process has to follow a FIFA important rule: it is not allowed to have more than one team from the same confederation in the same group (with the exception of UEFA, which is allowed to have a maximum of two teams in the same group). As a result, if we draw Poland for group C and group C already has Spain and Croatia, Poland will be allocated in the group D. In case group D also already has two European teams, Poland allocation will move to group E, and so on, until a group that fits the rules is found. 

![][image2]  
**Figure II.** Description of the group draw process 

	For each pseudo group draw created for the 2022 World Cup, we can predict the tournament from the group stage to the final match.

3. ## Tournament

	The tournament can be divided into six major blocks: the group stage, the round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals, third-place match, and final match (Figure III). As indicated in the World Cup Process section, in the group stage, all teams play each other, and points are accumulated to define the two teams that will move to the following round. Each victory is worth 3 points, ties are worth 1 point, and losses 0 points. In the case of a tie in the group ranking, other indicators are used to determine the teams moving to the knockout stage (e.g. difference of goals scored and goals taken, the total number of goals scored, and fair play).  
	In the knockout eliminatory stage, the winners of the groups play the runner-ups of the following alphabetical group (e.g. winner of group A plays the runner-up of group B). In the case of a match tie, there is an extra time and, if the tie persists, the teams go to the penalty shoot phase.  
	Each match of the tournament is predicted through a Poisson Regression. The model was trained using data from 1960 \- 2018, and the outcome of the model is the number of goals each team will score in a match. Given the number of goals scored by each team, for the group phase matches it’s possible to calculate the points, and for the knockout stages to directly calculate the teams moving up to the following rounds.

![][image3]  
**Figure III.** Description of the tournament stage

### *The Poisson Distribution*

     	The number of goals is modeled as a Poisson distribution, in which y represents the number of goals scored by team i against team j in match k at the world cup in year t. And the logarithm of the rate lambda is a regression that was estimated through the equation below.

      *(Equation I)* 

         The reason behind choosing a Poisson regression can be explained by how historically the number of goals in a match follows a Poisson distribution (instead of a normal distribution, for example). Figure IV shows this behavior comparing the number of goals in matches for the 1990, 1994, and 1998 World Cup, and a Poisson distribution with lambda 1.5.   
           The explanatory variables used to train the model are described in Table II. And the   
significance of these variables in the trained model is detailed in Table III. The results show how the difference in Elo scores between the team and the opponent is highly significant when predicting the number of goals the team will score. Moreover, the stage is significant with a positive coefficient, indicating that high stages are usually correlated with a higher number of goals. A reason for this might be due to the fact that ties are not allowed in the knockout stages, so different from the earlier group stages that allow for ties with zero goals, the higher stages will move extra-time and penalty shoots until a team scores more goals than its opponent.  
	Moreover, the variable for home continent advantage is also statistically significant. This significance indicates that if a team is playing in its home continent while its opponent is not, this team is likely to score more goals. This is an interesting finding once, earlier research done in Predicting the World Cup 2014 indicated that playing in ones’ home continent is not a statistically significant variable in itself, but it seems to become statistically significant when we change the variable to be a comparison between the team and its opponent. In other words, Uruguay playing Argentina in Brazil doesn’t have a home continent advantage since both are from South America, but Uruguay playing Switzerland in Brazil seems to benefit from a home continent advantage because of the difference between the team and the opponent home continent.

| Table II: Explanatory Variables Variables Description Cup Year The year that the cup occurred. This was used as a predictor once the literature (Goldman 2014\) indicates that the number of goals per match reduced over the years.  Diff Elo The difference between team i and team j (opponent) Elos ratings in the previous year (t-1) Avg Goals Scored by Team The average goals scored per match by team i in the year t-1  Avg Goals Taken by Opponent The average goals taken per match by team j (the opponent team) Tournament Stage The tournament stage (1 being the group phase, and 6 being the final match) Home Continent Advantage Categorical variable to indicate whether the team has home continent advantage \- which happens only when the team is playing in its home continent and the opponent team is not  | Table III: Regression Coefficients λ Intercept 1.915  (0.580) Cup Year \-0.001 (0.555) Diff Elo 0.002 (\< 2e-16) Avg Goals Scored  by Team 0.084 (0.234) Avg Goals Taken by Opp 0.031 (0.665) Tournament Stage 0.445 (0.023) Home Advantage 0.125  (0.001) Num. observations 1,400 AIC 4,393.8 Estimation: Maximum likelihood Estimation period: 1960 \- 2018 P-values are in parentheses   |
| :---- | ----- |

	At this point, it is important to distinguish between the Poisson regression result and a draw from the Poisson distribution in the context of the Monte Carlo simulation. After training the model, we have the coefficients for each variable, and with the coefficients, it’s possible to design a probability mass function (PMF) for each match. Details about the probability mass function of a Poisson distribution, alongside the mathematical interpretation of the coefficients, can be found in the Appendix. As a result, let’s imagine a simulation for the 2018 group match between Belgium and Tunisia. Given the explanatory variables and the coefficients from the trained model, it’s possible to plot a PMF of goals that Belgium will score against Tunisia in that match, and a PMF of goals that Tunisia will score against Belgium in that match (Figure V). 

           Once we have the PMFs, we can draw one point from each distribution (a point representing the number of goals scored). Points closer to the PMF peak are more likely to happen once they have a higher probability. Figure V shows how the curve for Belgium is shifted to the right and Tunisia’s curve is highly shifted to the left centered around zero. This indicates that a higher number of goals is more likely for Belgium and, consequently, Belgium is more likely to win. The figure also shows the result of a simulation in which the results were points A (for Tunisia) and B (for Belgium). In that simulation, Belgium scored 3 goals and Tunisia 1 goal. Just for comparison, the actual result of the match in 2018 was 5x2 for Belgium, thus Belgium was indeed the winner.  
	Before moving to 2022 World Cup predictions, it is relevant to test this framework in a World Cup that has already happened and check the results of the model with what actually happened in the tournament. The following section will validate this methodology simulating the 2018 World Cup.

# V.	Validation with the 2018 World Cup 

In order to validate the model performance, the 2018 World Cup tournament stage was simulated \- considering that for the 2018 World Cup we already know the teams that qualified and the group allocation. Analogous to what will be done to predict the 2022 World Cup, the 2018 tournament was simulated a thousand times and, in order to avoid in-sample bias, the Poisson model was re-trained removing the 2018 World Cup matches. The goal of predicting the 2018 World Cup is to check how close the teams that indeed moved up in each round probabilistically had the chance to move up according to the Poisson model and the Monte Carlo simulation. 

1. ## Group Stage

	The group stage result was simulated given the 2018 group draw. Figure VI shows the predicted winner and runner-up of each group compared to the actual results. The simulation correctly predicted 11 out of the 16 teams that moved to the round of 16 (green boxes). Some of the incorrect predictions were also surprising results at the time of the 2018 World Cup. For example, Germany, predicted in our simulation to be the winner of group F, was actually ranked last, which was also received with great surprise by the public. The team fans had never seen Germany be eliminated in the first round of the World Cup, and NYT headline at the time equally declared the surprise stating “Germany Crashes out of the World Cup”[^6].   
![][image4]  
**Figure VI.** Comparison between simulated and actual group phase results

2. ## Knockout Stages (Round of 16 Onwards)

	Additionally to the close predictions of the group stage, the knockout stages were also closely predicted. Table IV shows the percentage of times in the simulations that each team was present in the round. For instance, in the 1,000 simulations, Brazil was present in the round of 16 88% of the time. The highlighted grey cells indicate the teams that were in fact present in that round in the 2018 World Cup. Interpreting the table and the grey cells, we can see how the teams that were more likely to appear in the rounds based on the simulations were in fact the teams that appeared in the rounds during the real tournament. An interesting surprise was Sweden, that although didn’t seem to have a high chance of moving up the tournament rounds, made it to the quarter-finals.   
	The expected unpredictability of the final stages can also be seen in the table. Brazil was the champion 18% of the time in the simulations, Argentina was the champion 14% of the time, and France, which was the actual winner, was the winner in 9% of the simulations, which is still significantly higher compared to the other 32 teams. These predictions can also be compared to Goldman Sachs’s 2018 World Cup Report, which indicated Brazil to have a winning probability of 18.5%, and France to have a winning probability of 11%. Goldman Sachs’s report is written after the group draw and knowing which matches will happen, and the fact that the results are incredibly close to the approach proposed in this paper is a good indicator of the robustness of the proposed framework.  
We can then conclude that the combination of the Poisson Model with the Monte Carlo simulation provides valuable insights into the chances of each team moving up the rounds, and these chances, despite the surprises that happen during the World Cup, are good indicators of what might actually happen in the tournament. 

**Table IV**: World Cup 2018 Simulation (ordered based on the round of 16 probabilities)

| Teams | Round of 16 | Quarter-finals | Semi-finals | Final | Champion |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Brazil | 88.5% | 57.2% | 39.8% | 25.8% | 17.8% |
| Argentina | 86.1% | 58.9% | 40.1% | 24.9% | 13.7% |

# 

# 

# VI.	2022 World Cup Predictions

1. ## Qualifiers

	Using the FIFA ranking strategy described in the Analysis framework section, 32 teams were selected as the 32 qualified teams for the 2022 Qatar World Cup. The teams are displayed in Table V, and as a sanity check, we can see they are reasonable estimations for what we expect the qualified teams to be, both because they are teams that have been to previous World Cups and because of the ongoing qualifying process of each confederation. For example, given the stage of the qualifiers, we already know that Brazil, Argentina, Mexico, and the United State are teams that are qualified to go to the 2022 World Cup.

**Table V**. Estimated qualified teams for the 2022 World Cup

| UEFA |  |  | CAF |  | CONMEBOL |  | AFC |  | CONCACAF |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Belgium | Denmark |  | Senegal |  | Brazil |  | Japan |  | Mexico |
| France | Germany |  | Tunisia |  | Argentina |  | Iran |  | United States |
| England | Netherlands |  | Algeria |  | Uruguay |  | Korea  |  | Jamaica |
| Portugal | Switzerland |  | Morocco |  | Colombia |  | Australia |  | Costa Rica |
| Spain | Wales |  | Nigeria |  | Chile |  | Qatar |  |  |
| Italy | Poland |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Croatia |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

2. ## Knockout stages

   

	The groups are then randomized and the matches are predicted using the Monte Carlo and Poisson Model framework. Table VI shows the percentage of times each was present in the respective stage. Based on the results, three countries seem to be the favorites for the 2022 championship: Brazil, Belgium, and France. Analogous to what was done for the 2018 World Cup, this table can be used to estimate the likelihood each team moves to each round. For example, it’s extremely likely that Brazil will reach Round of 16 regardless of which group the team falls into after the group allocation \- in 88% of the simulations, accounting for randomized groups, Brazil was present in the round of 16\. On the flip side, it is unlikely that Jamaica will be in the semi-finals \- in only 0.4% of the simulations, accounting for randomized groups, Jamaica reached the semi-final stage.

| Teams | Round of 16 | Quarter-finals | Semi-finals | Final | Champion |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Brazil | 88.2% | 60.3% | 43.3% | 28.5% | 18.7% |

| Tunisia | 14.8% | 3.0% | 0.7% | 0.1% | 0.0% |
| Jamaica | 9.9% | 2.1% | 0.4% | 0.0% | 0.0% |

# VII.	Conclusion 

FIFA World Cup is a highly televised event that engages millions of soccer fans around the globe. This paper proposed a holistic framework that simulates the World Cup starting from the qualifiers and going until the final match. This framework allows us to make World Cup predictions before we know which teams are necessarily going to qualify and before the official group draw. The framework estimates the qualifying process based on FIFA rankings, creates randomized group draws following the FIFA rules, and predicts the tournament stages for each randomized group using Poisson Regressions.   
The approach was tested for the 2018 World Cup, and it correctly predicted 11 out of the 16 teams that qualified for the round of 16\. Moreover, for the following rounds, it correctly indicated the teams that were more likely to reach each stage \- with Germany performing worse than expected and not qualifying for the round of 16, and Sweden performing better than expected reaching the quarter-finals.   
For the 2022 World Cup, the estimation of the qualified teams was done using FIFA rankings, a thousand randomized groups were drawn, and the tournament for each randomized group was predicted. Table VI encapsulates the results, indicating Brazil to be the most likely to win the championship, followed by Belgium and France.  
Further work could be done to include a more robust algorithm to predict the qualifiers. For instance, the qualified teams could be estimated through another Poisson regression trained with historical data from each confederation qualifying process. Analogously, further work could include a variable that encapsulates individual player data in order to account for differences in the lineup during the tournament. Moreover, throughout the 2022 World Cup, the predictions from this paper could be updated as information becomes available (e.g. after the qualified teams are defined, after the group draw, and after each tournament round) and such updates would allow fans to have updated winning probabilities for their teams after each round.

Mathematical Appendix

1. Poisson Probability Mass Function (PMF)

![][image5]

2. Finding Poisson Coefficients through Maximum Likelihood

In order to get the log-likelihood, we multiply all the PMFs for the individual observations i (in our case, each team score yi).  
![][image6]

Each λi can be different and expressed by the previously derived Equation II:   
![][image7]  
We can then re-write the log-likelihood function using the function λ for each i and find for which values of the coefficients (β) the log-likelihood is maximized.

A more detailed explanation can be found on Roback, Paul, and Julie Legler. “Chapter 4: Poisson Regression.” Beyond Multiple Linear Regression: Applied Generalized Linear Models And Multilevel Models in R.

3. Interpreting Poisson Coefficients

 Poisson regression coefficients are interpreted as the difference between the log of expected counts, not as incremental units for the dependent variable. Derivation below.

![][image8]

# References

1. Goldman Sachs. “The World Cup and Economics.” (2014)  
     
2. Groll, Andreas et al. “Prediction of major international soccer tournaments based on team-specific regularized Poisson regression: An application to the FIFA World Cup 2014.” Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports 11 (2015): 115 \- 97\.  
     
3. Groll, Andreas & Ley, Christophe & Schauberger, Gunther & Eetvelde, Hans. “Prediction of the FIFA World Cup 2018 \- A random forest approach with an emphasis on estimated team ability parameters.” (2018)   
     
4. Carsey, Thomas M., and Jeffrey J. Harden. “Chapter 6: Simulating Generalized Linear Models.” Monte Carlo Simulation and Resampling Methods for Social Science, Sage, Los Angeles, 2014\.   
     
5. Roback, Paul, and Julie Legler. “Chapter 4: Poisson Regression.” Beyond Multiple Linear Regression: Applied Generalized Linear Models And Multilevel Models in R, 1st ed., Chapman and Hall/CRC, 2020\.  
     
6. Becklas, Andre (2017). Dataset: World Cup Matches, Version 5\. Retrieved October 2021 from https://www.kaggle.com/abecklas/fifa-world-cup.  
     
7. World Football Elo Ratings. Retrieved October 2021 from [https://www.eloratings.net/](https://www.eloratings.net/)

8. More than half the world watched record-breaking 2018 World Cup. Retrieved December 2021 from [https://www.fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup/2018russia/media-releases/more-than-half-the-world-watched-record-breaking-2018-world-cup](https://www.fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup/2018russia/media-releases/more-than-half-the-world-watched-record-breaking-2018-world-cup)  
     
9. Goldman Sachs. “The World Cup and Economics.” (2018)

   

[^1]:  FIFA news (fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup)

[^2]:  The number of qualified teams per confederation may change depending on the intercontinental playoffs

[^3]:  UEFA (uefa.com/european-qualifiers)

[^4]:  Considering Russia was automatically qualified as the World Cup host

[^5]:  FIFA rankings of October 2017 considered 

[^6]:  The New York Times, 2018 (https://www.nytimes.com/2018/06/27/sports/world-cup)
